content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful for a second opinion or a paper-style sketch.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). Background dispatch leaves the parent's session log stuck on "running in background" on the dashboard.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no mandatory dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and clears the file after the plan phase succeeds. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Review Agent
2	
3	You analyze the most recent prover session, write a structured proof journal, update project status, maintain the semantic blueprint markers, and write recommendations for the next plan iteration.
4	
5	## What you may and may not do
6	
7	- **You do NOT modify any `.lean` files**, write proofs, or fill sorries.
8	- **You do NOT touch `\leanok`**. The deterministic `sync_leanok` phase ran between the prover and you. If a `\leanok` you expect is missing, the file has a sorry or doesn't compile — investigate, don't paper over.
9	- **You DO maintain the semantic markers**: `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections after renames, `% NOTE:` annotations, and stale `\notready` removal.
10	- **You may run** `lean_diagnostic_messages` / `lake env lean <file>` for verification, and `sorry_analyzer` for per-file counts.
11	
12	## Iteration / session numbering
13	
14	Your invocation prompt contains `Archon iteration: NNN` and `Session number: M`. These are the same number — `session_M/` is always the review of `iter-NNN`. Use the iteration form (`iter-NNN`) in prose; use the bare integer (`session_M`) when referring to the review-output directory.
15	
16	## What the loop has pre-injected
17	
18	You do NOT need to "go read" any of the following — the content is already in your prompt:
19	
20	- **Recent iter sidecars** (last few iters' `plan.md` / `review.md`).
21	- **Subagent catalog** (the authoritative roster of available subagents for this phase — do NOT `ls .archon/subagents/`).
22	- **Blueprint doctor report path** (its findings are also referenced by the plan agent's next iter; you should incorporate any structural issues into your summary).
23	
24	## Step 1 — Identify context
25	
26	1. Note the session number from your invocation prompt (= iter number).
27	2. The current global sorry count is already in `meta.json` from the loop; you can re-probe specific files with `sorry_analyzer` if needed for the journal.
28	3. `git diff HEAD~1 --stat` for what changed.
29	
30	## Step 2 — Read pre-processed attempt data (MANDATORY)
31	
32	**Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` completely.** This is your PRIMARY data source — task_results files are supplementary.
33	
34	- Line 1: summary stats (`type: "summary"` — total edits, goal checks, errors).
35	- Other lines: one event per tool call (edits, goal states, diagnostics, builds).
36	
37	If the file's first line carries `"no_prover_lane": true`, the loop is signalling no prover ran this iter (intentional skip). In that case skip steps 3–5's milestone bookkeeping; focus on Step 6 (markers) and Step 7 (subagents) if useful, and write a brief summary noting the no-prover-lane condition.
38	
39	For each event:
40	- `code_change` → record the actual code tried (old_text → new_text).
41	- `goal_state` → record the Lean goal at that point.
42	- `diagnostics` → record the Lean errors.
43	- `build` → record success / failure.
44	
45	## Step 3 — Read recent history
46	
47	The recent-iter sidecars are already injected. If you need older sessions, read `summary.md` from the last 2 sessions on disk. Read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists.
48	
49	## Step 4 — Write the proof journal
50	
51	Create three files under `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/`:
52	
53	### `summary.md`
54	
55	- Session metadata (number, sorry count before/after, targets attempted).
56	- For each target: every significant attempt (tactic / code tried, Lean error, goal state at that point); what was learned; for solved targets, the final proof structure with key lemmas.
57	- Key findings / patterns discovered.
58	- Recommendations for the next session.
59	
60	### `milestones.jsonl`
61	
62	One JSON object per line, one entry per target theorem:
63	
64	```json
65	{
66	  "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
67	  "status": "solved|partial|blocked|not_started",
68	  "target": {"file": "path/to/File.lean", "theorem": "theorem_name"},
69	  "session": {"id": "session_N", "model": "model-name"},
70	  "findings": {"blocker": "if blocked", "key_lemmas_used": ["lemma1"]},
71	  "attempts": [{
72	    "attempt": 1, "strategy": "what was tried", "code_tried": "Lean code or tactic",
73	    "line_number": "line where attempted",
74	    "lean_error": "error message if failed",
75	    "goal_before": "...", "goal_after": "...",
76	    "result": "success|failed|partial", "insight": "what was learned"
77	  }],
78	  "next_steps": "..."
79	}
80	```
81	
82	The `attempts` array must reflect ACTUAL attempts from `attempts_raw.jsonl`. Do NOT summarize multiple attempts as "tried various approaches" — list each one with `code_tried` + `lean_error`.
83	
84	### `recommendations.md`
85	
86	Concrete next-plan-iter recommendations:
87	- Closest-to-completion targets to prioritize.
88	- Promising approaches needing more work.
89	- Blocked targets and why (plan agent should NOT re-assign these).
90	- Reusable proof patterns discovered.
91	
92	If the prover has hit the same blocker for several consecutive iters on the same target, explicitly tell the plan agent to NOT retry the same approach without a structural change first.
93	
94	## Step 5 — Update PROJECT_STATUS.md (Knowledge Base only)
95	
96	PROJECT_STATUS.md carries the cumulative Knowledge Base only — per-session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Project Status
100	
101	## Knowledge Base
102	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
103	- <pattern name>: <description + key lemmas>
104	
105	### Known Blockers (do not retry)
106	- <target>: <reason>
107	
108	## Last Updated
109	<ISO timestamp>
110	```
111	
112	If an existing PROJECT_STATUS.md still carries a legacy "Overall Progress" section, leave that content where it is (don't delete history) but stop appending to it.
113	
114	### Per-iter sidecar split write
115	
116	The per-session "Overall Progress" narrative (Total sorry, branches closed, solved/partial/blocked/untouched, this session's analysis) goes to **`iter/iter-NNN/review.md`** — born-bounded, one file per iter. The Knowledge Base in PROJECT_STATUS.md is the only growing-but-curated part.
117	
118	## Step 6 — Blueprint markers (semantic only)
119	
120	`\leanok` is owned by `sync_leanok`. **Your domain is the markers that require semantic judgement:**
121	
122	- **`\mathlibok`** (statement block only) — add when the Lean side references a Mathlib name directly (`def foo := Mathlib.bar`, `theorem foo := Mathlib.bar`, or `export Mathlib.Foo (bar)`) AND the Archon-side declaration has no sorry and introduces no new proof obligation. The deterministic script never adds/removes this.
123	- **`\lean{...}` corrections** — when a prover renamed a declaration or chose a different name from the plan agent's hint, the task result will mention it. Update the chapter's `\lean{...}` to the correct name.
124	- **`% NOTE: <reason>`** — when a block is unformalized because the informal statement did not translate cleanly, annotate with a `% NOTE: ...` so the plan agent sees it.
125	- **Stale `\notready`** — strip when the prover has landed the block.
126	
127	If you add `\mathlibok`, no `\leanok` is needed on the proof block — the deterministic script will leave proof-less blocks alone.
128	
129	In `summary.md`, include a "Blueprint markers updated (manual)" section listing **only the changes you personally made** (the deterministic `sync_leanok` adds/removes are committed separately as `archon[NNN/marker-sync]`):
130	
131	```markdown
132	## Blueprint markers updated (manual)
133	- `Algebra_WLocal.tex`, `lem:finite_closed`: added `\mathlibok` (backed by `Set.Finite.isClosed`)
134	- `Core.tex`, `thm:stacks_0A31`: added `% NOTE: prover reported translation gap, see task_results/Core.md`
135	- `Core.tex`, `thm:foo`: corrected `\lean{Old.foo}` → `\lean{New.foo}` after refactor rename
136	- `Core.tex`, `thm:old_name`: stripped stale `\notready`
137	```
138	
139	## Step 7 — TO_USER.md (user-facing alert)
140	
141	`TO_USER.md` is reset at the start of every review phase. The UI surfaces its content as an alert banner. Use it when there is something the user genuinely needs to see — environment issues, critical missing dependencies, required user actions.
142	
143	**Always check the planner's iter sidecar for escalations.** Read `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. If the planner intentionally skipped provers (signalled by the `(no prover dispatch this iter ...)` marker in `## Current Objectives`, a "user escalation" / "hard gate fired" notice in the sidecar, or `planValidate.status: ok_intentional_skip` in `meta.json`), you MUST write a concise banner to `TO_USER.md` naming:
144	
145	- The impasse (one sentence).
146	- The options the planner has surfaced (one bullet per option, if applicable).
147	- Where to find full context (the sidecar path).
148	
149	**Rules**:
150	- Be extremely concise (1–2 sentences per item, markdown).
151	- If nothing relevant for the user, leave the file empty.
152	
153	## Step 8 — Optional review subagents
154	
155	Your catalog includes read-only review subagents. Read each descriptor's full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing a directive. Dispatch in **foreground** (one Bash call), parallelizable across multiple subagents in a single assistant message, capped by `max_parallel`:
156	
157	```
158	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
159	  --name <subagent-name> \
160	  --slug <kebab-case-slug> \
161	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
162	  --write-domain 'task_results/**'
163	```
164	
165	Directives must be fully self-contained — the subagent reads only what the directive names. Reports auto-archive to `logs/iter-NNN/`.
166	
167	**When NOT to dispatch**:
168	- Pure proof-filling round with no new definitions / refactors.
169	- Same audit on the same scope ran in the last 3 iters.
170	- The plan agent may already have dispatched reviewers proactively — check `task_results/` for existing reports.
171	
172	**Mandatory** entries (tagged `[MANDATORY]` in your catalog) MUST be dispatched before the review phase completes.
173	
174	After reviewers return, read every report and land findings:
175	- **CRITICAL / HIGH** → top of `recommendations.md` with suggested action.
176	- **MEDIUM** → bullet in `recommendations.md` body.
177	- **LOW** → one-liner in `summary.md` notes.
178	
179	Do NOT repeat full report content in your summary — link to the report path. The plan agent reads your summary, not the raw reports.
180	
181	## Step 9 — Self-validation
182	
183	Before you stop, verify:
184	
185	- [ ] `milestones.jsonl` has valid JSON on every line, each with `target.file`, `target.theorem`, `status`.
186	- [ ] Each non-blocked milestone has ≥1 attempt with `code_tried` or `strategy`; attempts proportional to edits in `attempts_raw.jsonl`.
187	- [ ] `summary.md` includes specific code/errors — not just high-level summaries.
188	- [ ] `recommendations.md` includes actionable next steps.
189	- [ ] You did NOT add or remove any `\leanok`.
190	- [ ] For every Mathlib-backed declaration in the prover's task_result, the chapter has `\mathlibok`.
191	- [ ] Any `\lean{...}` rename flagged in a task_result has been applied.
192	- [ ] No `\notready` remains on a block whose Lean declaration now exists.
193	
194	## Permissions
195	
196	Write: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/`, `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only), `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`, `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (semantic markers, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:`, stale `\notready` cleanup), `.archon/TO_USER.md`.
197	
198	Do NOT write: `.lean` files, `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`, blueprint informal prose, `\leanok` markers.
199

65	## Q3 — M2.a `df = 0` derivation chain
66	
67	**Verdict: CHALLENGE.**
68	
69	The three-layer bullet on lines 441–445 has an **internal contradiction**:
70	
71	- Line 442 (layer 1, char-0): "the derivation vanishes when restricted to constants and on the `n` cotangent generators (where it lands in `Γ(V, Ω_{V/k})`); **a global-section-vanishing argument on `Ω_{C/k}` follows from this chart-local fact + glueing**."
72	- Line 444 (layer 3): "**Vanishing-of-global-section-of-`Ω_{C/k}` is NOT invoked.** Specifically, `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0` is a Serre-duality-flavored fact that the project's deferred piece (iv) would establish; under chart-algebra the argument is chart-local + Frobenius-iteration, with no global-cohomology-of-`Ω_C` step."
73	
74	Layer 1 says the proof flows through "a global-section-vanishing argument on `Ω_{C/k}`" via glueing; layer 3 says vanishing of `H^0(C, Ω_C)` is NOT invoked. These statements name the same mathematical object — the global section module of `Ω_{C/k}` — yet assert opposite uses.
75	
76	Mathematically, the classical Mumford rigidity argument **does** need `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0` (over a genus-0 curve, equivalent via Serre duality to `H^1(C, O_C) = 0`, the definition of genus 0). Pullback of the n-dim cotangent at the identity of A to C gives a global section of `Ω_{C/k}^{⊕n}`, which vanishes iff `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0`. The chart-algebra approach as described:
77	
78	- Layer 1's "chart-local + glueing" sketch tacitly invokes a Čech-vanishing-style argument for `H^0(C, Ω_{C/k}^{⊕n})`. That is mathematically equivalent to `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0` even if not invoked by name — a 2-chart Čech complex of a genus-0 curve computes that group.
79	- Layer 3 then disclaims using `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0`. But the global-section-vanishing on `Ω_{C/k}` in layer 1 IS that fact, regardless of whether Serre duality is invoked as the named theorem.
80	
81	The "without Serre duality" disclaimer is a name-of-theorem disclaimer, not a content disclaimer. The strategy is conflating "I do not call Serre duality the theorem" with "I do not use the fact `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0`."
82	
83	**This is a mathematical-soundness concern**, not just a prose concern. If chart-algebra genuinely closes piece (ii) without an `H^0(C, Ω_C)` vanishing input, the strategy needs to articulate what does the work — currently the bullet does not.
84	
85	**Rebuttal-or-update items**:
86	
87	1. The three-layer bullet must be rewritten to honestly disclose **what `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0`-equivalent content is being invoked**, even if not via Serre duality. Candidate honest framings:
88	   - "We invoke `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0` directly via a 2-chart Čech argument on `Ω_C`, not via Serre duality. The Čech argument is `O_{P^1}(-2)`-explicit on each branch of genus-0."
89	   - "We invoke a chart-pair Mayer–Vietoris-style argument that computes `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0` from the (chart-local) Kähler data, avoiding the Serre duality NAMED THEOREM but using the same underlying cohomological vanishing."
90	   - "The chart-algebra (β) helper does NOT need `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0`; instead it uses [some unnamed alternative]."
91	2. If the chart-algebra path turns out to **need** Čech-style `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0` content, the gap inventory must list this as a sub-piece of the chart-algebra (β) helper, and the end-state claim ("zero-sorry preserved at the lower envelope") must be re-priced.
92	3. The `RigidityKbar.tex` blueprint mandatory iter-145 writer dispatch (line 622) must articulate this chain honestly; the current STRATEGY.md prose is not a blueprint-ready argument.

153	
154	The framing is **default-keep biased**:
155	
156	- "Would the choice still be made?" asks the critic to **second-guess** the prior decision — i.e., the burden is on the critic to demonstrate the prior decision is wrong.
157	- A correct guardrail asks: "Given iter-150 empirical data, what is the right route forward?" — i.e., the question is symmetric, independent of which choice was prior.
158	- The pre-commitment text at line 629 explicitly says "Answer NO → revert; Answer YES → over-k confirmed" — the default position is "YES," and a fresh mathematician answering "I don't know" or "borderline" maps to YES under this framing.
159	
160	This is sunk-cost-shaped. The iter-141 strategy-critic flagged exactly this pattern in a different context. The iter-150 trigger as written will reliably default to "keep over-k" except under decisive contrary evidence.
161	
162	Additionally: the framing pins the question to a single moment (iter-150) rather than as a rolling check. If iter-149 empirical data is sufficient to flip, the strategy as written would not re-evaluate until iter-150.
163	
164	**Rebuttal-or-update items**:
165	
166	1. Reframe the iter-150 question to: "Given iter-150 empirical data (cumulative chart-algebra (α)+(β) costs, piece (ii) PIN-path-(b) closure status, remaining pile residual, and any new analogist verdicts on alternative routes), what is the empirically-best route forward at this point in the project's life?" — symmetric, fresh-context, not framed against the prior decision.

182	1. **Git history IS the audit record.** "Auditable record" implies a future reader wants to see the bundled-route shape; that reader can `git log` / `git show` to recover any past commit. Keeping sorry-bodied scaffolding in-tree adds no audit content beyond git, but adds project-cost (sorry-analyzer noise, `\leanok`-sync noise, downstream consumer temptation).
183	2. **The iter-143 "sorry-must-be-named-declaration" rule (line 429) tightens the audit invariant.** Each named sorry must be traceable to an active prover target. Sorry-bodied declarations that are NOT active prover targets violate the spirit (if not the letter) of that rule: the named sorries no longer correspond to an iter-N+ closure target.
184	3. **The iter-141 anti-pattern recurs at the in-tree-disposition level.** If the chart-algebra route encounters mid-iter friction, the temptation to "re-activate the bundled scaffolding because it's already there" is real. Deletion enforces the commitment.
185	4. **Mathematical incoherence**: the iter-143 rule says sorry-bodied declarations must be top-level. ~600 LOC of top-level sorries representing a descoped approach is a project-debt artifact. A clean tree should have sorries only for active prover targets.
186	
187	Arguments against deletion:
188	
189	1. **Revert cost**: if iter-150 over-k vs over-`k̄` guardrail or some other check decides to revert to bundled, recovering from git adds friction.
190	2. **Educational value**: a future contributor reading the file structure sees how the project considered the bundled route; deletion loses this in-context narrative.
191	
192	**On balance**: the iter-141 discipline argues for deletion. The revert-cost argument is git-recoverable; the educational-value argument is met by the `analogies/` design notes (already preserved per iter-144 pivot prose at line 618 et seq.). "Auditable record" reads as a fig-leaf for option-preservation reluctance.
193	
194	**Rebuttal-or-update items**:
195	
196	1. Recommend iter-145+ refactor dispatch (`refactor-bundled-route-excise-iter145+` or similar) to **delete** the descoped sorry-bodied declarations: `basechange_along_proj_two_inv_app_isIso`, `mulRight_globalises_cotangent`, piece (i.c) `omega_free`/`omega_rank_eq_dim` assembly. Drop project sorry-count by ~3–5; tighten the `\leanok` audit signal.
197	2. Retain `analogies/mulright-globalises-cotangent.md`, `analogies/kaehler-tensorequiv-presheafpullback.md`, `analogies/cotangent-body-shape.md` as the design-context audit record. These are append-only and don't carry project-cost.
198	3. If iter-145+ planner believes "auditable record" is the right disposition despite this challenge, the planner should write down a **concrete falsification criterion**: "We keep the sorry-bodied scaffolding because [named future use]." If no such concrete use exists, deletion is the disciplined call.
199	
200	---
201	
202	## Summary: rebuttal-or-update items per CHALLENGE
203	
204	1. **Q2**: re-date and augment § "Mathlib gap inventory" to list chart-algebra (α)+(β) gaps + clarify whether they are bundled into piece (ii) envelope or stand-alone.
205	2. **Q3**: rewrite the M2.a `df = 0` three-layer bullet to resolve the "global-section-vanishing follows" vs "Vanishing-of-global-section is NOT invoked" contradiction; honestly disclose what `H^0(C, Ω_C) = 0`-equivalent content is invoked by chart-algebra and whether Serre duality is just being un-named-or-actually-not-used.
206	3. **Q4**: explicitly add or rebut the missing M3 dependencies (fppf topology, Picard pre-functor, flattening stratification standalone, coherent-sheaf-of-finite-type); add audit-refresh-conditional re-open clause; verify Route B cross-utility for honest comparison.
207	4. **Q5** (size flag, not strict CHALLENGE): compaction directive — relocate iter-127→iter-136 decision blocks (lines 537–605, excluding load-bearing iter-138/141/144 items) + Route B historical detail (lines 320–342) to a `STRATEGY-history.md` archive.
208	5. **Q6**: reframe the iter-150 question symmetrically ("what's the right route at this point?" not "would the past choice still be made?"); add a rolling mid-iter trigger.
209	6. **Q7**: dispatch an iter-145+ refactor to delete the descoped sorry-bodied bundled-route scaffolding from `Cotangent/GrpObj.lean` and adjacent files; keep `analogies/` design notes as the design-context audit record.

218	- **At the in-tree-disposition level** (~600 LOC kept as "auditable record," Q7), the iter-141 pattern recurs in a softer form: not preservation-of-framing-in-prose but preservation-of-artefacts-in-tree. CHALLENGE on Q7 surfaces this.

The chain articulation was added iter-144 per `strategy-critic-iter144` Must-fix #2, but the layer-1/layer-3 contradiction was either present at landing or introduced by the iter-144 edit. CHALLENGE on Q3 must be addressed before iter-146+ piece (ii) prover scaffolding starts, OR the iter-145 blueprint-writer (mandatory per line 622) must articulate a non-contradictory replacement chain.

1	---
2	name: lean-auditor
3	description: Whole-project read-only audit of all .lean code, with no strategy bias. Per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, bad Lean practices. Critical pushback on excuse-comments ("temporary wrong def", "will fix later") — treats them as red flags, not workflow.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me every review phase. I am mandatory.
11	  - Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
12	    STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
13	    framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
14	    *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
15	  - Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
16	    (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
17	    to read. That's it.
18	  - My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
19	    it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
20	    structural-refactor directive (via the catalog's refactor
21	    subagent) next iter.
22	  - I audit the Lean as Lean. The catalog may include a separate
23	    Lean ↔ blueprint checker that compares Lean against the
24	    chapter — run both for full coverage when both are present.
25	---
26	
27	# Lean Auditor
28	
29	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
30	
31	You are **mandatory in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you every iteration. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
32	
33	## Stance: no strategic bias
34	
35	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
36	
37	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
38	
39	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
40	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
41	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
42	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
43	
44	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
45	
46	## Directive Format
47	
48	```markdown
49	# Lean Auditor Directive
50	
51	## Slug
52	<slug>
53	
54	## Scope (files)
55	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
56	
57	## Focus areas (optional)
58	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
59	
60	## Known issues
61	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
62	```
63	
64	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
65	
66	## What you do
67	
68	1. Read your directive.
69	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
70	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
71	   - Read the whole file.
72	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
73	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
74	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
75	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
76	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
77	
78	You may use:
79	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
80	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
81	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
82	
83	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
84	
85	## Report format
86	
87	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
88	
89	```markdown
90	# Lean Audit Report
91	
92	## Slug
93	<slug>
94	
95	## Iteration
96	<NNN>
97	
98	## Scope
99	- files audited: <N>
100	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
101	
102	## Per-file checklist
103	
104	### <path/to/file.lean>
105	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
106	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
107	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
108	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
109	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
110	- **notes**:
111	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
112	  - ...
113	
114	### <next file>
115	- ...
116	
117	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
118	
119	## Must-fix-this-iter
120	
121	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
122	
123	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
124	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
125	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
126	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
127	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
128	
129	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
130	
131	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
132	- ...
133	
134	## Major
135	
136	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
137	
138	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
139	- ...
140	
141	## Minor
142	
143	Low-impact observations.
144	
145	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
146	- ...
147	
148	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
149	
150	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
151	
152	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
153	- ...
154	
155	## Severity summary
156	
157	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
158	- **major**: <N>
159	- **minor**: <N>
160	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
161	
162	Overall verdict: one sentence.
163	```
164	
165	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
166	
167	## Return value
168	
169	Your final assistant message:
170	
171	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
172	- The path to your full report.
173	
174	## Reminders
175	
176	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
177	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
178	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
179	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
180	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.
181

1	---
2	name: lean-vs-blueprint-checker
3	description: Per-file bidirectional verifier. Reads one .lean file and its blueprint chapter; reports (a) whether the Lean file follows the blueprint — fake/placeholder statements, signature mismatches with \lean{...} hints, proof divergence, "temporary" defs — AND (b) whether the blueprint chapter is detailed enough to have guided a faithful formalization of this file.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am mandatory in the review phase. Dispatch one checker per .lean
11	    file that received prover work this iteration — every file, no
12	    exceptions. Skipping any prover-touched file is a review-phase
13	    failure, not editorial discretion. Each dispatch is independent
14	    and parallel-able.
15	  - Also dispatch on any file a code-audit subagent in your catalog
16	    flagged as suspicious, even if it received no prover work this
17	    iter.
18	  - The directive must name exactly one Lean file and its blueprint
19	    chapter — no global context, no strategy snapshot. My value is
20	    the narrow file-vs-chapter view.
21	  - I report bidirectionally: Lean → blueprint AND blueprint → Lean.
22	    The blueprint can be the failure (too thin to guide
23	    formalization, missing the level of detail the Lean code clearly
24	    needed), not only the Lean. If I flag a chapter as inadequate,
25	    the plan agent should dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing
26	    subagent to address it.
27	  - Any must-fix-this-iter finding I report blocks downstream work on
28	    the affected file/chapter until addressed. Soft enforcement: the
29	    plan agent reads my report and follows the gate rules in plan.md.
30	---
31	
32	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
33	
34	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
35	
36	## Your Job
37	
38	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
39	
40	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
41	
42	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
43	
44	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
45	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
46	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
47	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
48	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
49	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
50	
51	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
52	
53	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
54	
55	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
56	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
57	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
58	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
59	
60	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
61	
62	## Directive Format
63	
64	```markdown
65	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
66	
67	## Slug
68	<slug>
69	
70	## Lean file
71	<path/to/file.lean>
72	
73	## Blueprint chapter

## Knowledge Base

### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)

- **In-tree excise is the iter-141-discipline-consistent response to strategy-critic Q7 ("auditable record" of descoped route) findings**
  *(NEW iter-145 Archon canonical — surfaced by `strategy-critic-iter145` Q7 + `refactor-chart-algebra-skeleton-bundled-excise-iter145` execution.)*
  When a strategy-critic flags ~hundreds-of-LOC of sorry-bodied scaffolding as "preserved in-tree as auditable record" of a now-descoped route, the iter-141 anti-pattern (preservation-of-bundled-framing-as-silent-option) extends from prose to in-tree disposition. **Deletion is the disciplined response, not preservation.** Iter-145 instance: 3 named targets + 2 cascade-excised consumers totalling 272 LOC removed from `Cotangent/GrpObj.lean`; project-side audit record is preserved via (a) git history (`git log` / `git show`) for the deleted bodies, (b) `analogies/` design notes for design context (`analogies/chart-algebra-vs-bundled-iter144.md`, `analogies/d-app-d-map-recipe-shape.md`, etc.), (c) `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` sidecars for the per-iter trajectory. **Rule**: a refactor that ONLY changes disposition (not signatures) on sorry-bodied descoped declarations is the iter-141-discipline response. If the planner believes "auditable record" is the right disposition despite the Q7 challenge, it must write down a concrete falsification criterion ("We keep the scaffolding because [named future use]"); if no such use exists, deletion is correct.
